section.title: The Sayings of King Lemuel
section.sectionRationale: A second distinct voice closes the book alongside Agur, King Lemuel, otherwise unknown, whose oracle is explicitly credited to the instruction his mother taught him. It warns a young ruler against squandering strength on women and wine, and calls him instead to open his mouth for the voiceless, the poor, and the needy.
section.thesis: A king's strength is not for indulgence in women or wine but for defending the rights of the oppressed, the poor, and the needy, a charge a mother pressed on her royal son and one every person entrusted with any authority is called to imitate.
section.frameNote: Two clusters: a mother's warning against squandered strength; a mother's charge to speak for the voiceless and judge righteously.

verses.text: These are the words of King Lemuel, the burden that his mother taught him:

What shall I say, O my son? What, O son of my womb? 
What, O son of my vows?
Do not spend your strength on women or your vigor on those who ruin kings.

It is not for kings, O Lemuel, it is not for kings to drink wine, 
or for rulers to crave strong drink,
lest they drink and forget what is decreed, depriving all the oppressed of justice.
Give strong drink to one who is perishing, and wine to the bitter in soul.
Let him drink and forget his poverty, and remember his misery no more.

units.label: A mother's charge
units.note: speak for the voiceless, judge righteously

verses.text: Open your mouth for those with no voice, for the cause of all the {{dal|dispossessed}}.
Open your mouth, judge righteously, and defend the cause of the poor and needy.

dal.gloss: Poor, weak, dispossessed, the socially and economically vulnerable. Verse 8 charges the king to open his mouth for this class of people who have no voice of their own, an obligation that runs throughout the Torah and the Prophets and finds its ultimate model in the true King who came to bring good news to the poor.

basic.context: This short oracle preserves a mother's instruction to her royal son, King Lemuel, otherwise unknown in Scripture. She warns him plainly against squandering his strength on women who ruin kings and against the drunkenness that would make him forget justice, and then charges him with his true calling: to open his mouth for the voiceless, the poor, and the needy.
basic.hermeneutics: This is direct, personal instruction from a mother to a son who happens to be a king, applying with particular force to anyone in a position of authority, though its call to defend the voiceless and needy binds every reader regardless of rank. The permission to give strong drink to the perishing, verses 6 to 7, is not a general endorsement of drunkenness but a specific, almost medicinal exception contrasted sharply with the king's own required sobriety.
basic.originalLanguages: The dal, poor or dispossessed, verse 8, names those without social power to advocate for themselves, the very reason a king's mouth, his authority and voice, must be opened on their behalf. The mother's threefold repetition, 'my son... son of my womb... son of my vows,' verse 2, intensifies the urgency and intimacy of her charge, a vow apparently made to God concerning this son before his birth.
basic.typology: Lemuel's charge to open his mouth for the voiceless and defend the poor and needy, verses 8-9, anticipates the ministry of Christ, who was anointed to preach good news to the poor, Luke 4:18, and who is the true King whose reign is finally marked by perfect justice for the oppressed. The warning against a king's self-indulgence, verses 3-5, anticipates the greater King who, though entitled to everything, emptied Himself rather than indulge Himself, Philippians 2:6-7.
basic.christ: Lemuel's charge to open his mouth for the voiceless and defend the poor and needy, verses 8-9, is fulfilled in Jesus, the true King who announced His mission as bringing good news to the poor and setting the oppressed free, Luke 4:18, and who consistently used His voice and authority on behalf of those with none of their own. And where Lemuel is warned against squandering royal strength on self-indulgence, verses 3-5, Christ, though truly a king entitled to glory, emptied Himself and took the form of a servant, Philippians 2:6-7, the perfect reversal of every self-serving ruler this oracle warns against.
basic.god: God's own concern for the voiceless, the poor, and the needy stands behind this charge to the king, since Scripture consistently reveals a God who hears the cry of the oppressed and holds rulers accountable for how they treat the powerless. He also cares about the ordinary integrity of a ruler's private life, strength, sobriety, and self-control, not merely public policy, since character and justice cannot finally be separated.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 4:18 fulfills the charge to bring good news to the poor. Philippians 2:6-7 fulfills the pattern of a king who empties himself rather than indulging himself. Psalm 72:1-4 echoes a king's calling to defend the afflicted and needy. Isaiah 1:17 echoes the charge to defend the cause of the oppressed.
basic.oneStory: A mother's charge to her son the king, to guard his strength, his sobriety, and above all his voice on behalf of the voiceless, sketches the pattern of righteous rule the whole Bible is waiting for. That pattern is fulfilled in Jesus, the true King who did not squander His glory in self-indulgence but poured Himself out to bring justice and good news to the poor.

context.literary: This short passage, 31:1-9, forms a distinct closing oracle alongside the words of Agur in chapter 30, both voices standing apart from the earlier Solomonic material, and both explicitly attributed to named but otherwise unknown speakers, here a queen mother instructing her royal son.
context.historical: The identity of King Lemuel and his unnamed mother remain unknown outside this text; some ancient Jewish tradition identified Lemuel with Solomon under another name, though this cannot be established with certainty, and the passage's content, a queen mother's political and moral instruction to her royal son, fits the broader ancient Near Eastern pattern of royal wisdom literature.
context.canonical: This oracle's concern for kings defending the poor and needy rather than indulging themselves echoes the ideal royal portrait of Psalm 72 and anticipates the prophetic critique of Israel's actual kings who so often failed this standard, setting up the longing for a truly righteous king fulfilled in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To preserve a mother's urgent instruction to her royal son, warning him against self-indulgence that corrupts justice and charging him to use his voice and authority for the voiceless, the poor, and the needy.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage is almost entirely direct prescription, a mother's commands to her son, do not spend your strength on women, it is not for kings to drink wine, open your mouth for the voiceless, making it one of the more straightforwardly applicable passages in the closing chapters of Proverbs.

debates.question: Do verses 6-7, giving strong drink to the perishing and bitter in soul, endorse using alcohol to numb suffering as a general practice?

views.label: Yes, it commends alcohol as a legitimate comfort for the suffering
views.case: The verses' plain sense offers wine and strong drink specifically to ease the suffering of the perishing and bitter in soul, suggesting a limited but real endorsement of alcohol's numbing effect in extreme distress.

views.label: It is a specific contrast highlighting the king's necessary sobriety, not a general endorsement of numbing pain with alcohol
views.case: The passage's structure sets this exception directly against the king's own required abstinence, verses 4-5, precisely because the king must never forget what is decreed or the cause of the oppressed; the point is not a general prescription for using alcohol to cope with suffering but a rhetorical contrast that sharpens why sobriety matters uniquely for those who must render justice.

debates.bearing: Reading verses 6-7 as a contrast rather than a general practical recommendation keeps the passage's focus where it belongs, on the unique responsibility of those with authority to remain clear-headed for the sake of justice, without turning an ancient rhetorical device into medical or pastoral counsel for handling grief.

study.originalLanguages: The dal, poor or dispossessed, verse 8, names those without social standing to speak for themselves, making the king's own mouth, his authority and voice, the necessary instrument of their defense. The mother's intense, threefold address, 'my son, son of my womb, son of my vows,' verse 2, reflects the weight of a vow made concerning this child, intensifying the urgency of the charge that follows.

typology.type: Opening the mouth for the voiceless and needy, 31:8-9
typology.fulfillment: Christ's mission to preach good news to the poor and set the oppressed free, Luke 4:18.

typology.type: The warning against a king's self-indulgence, 31:3-5
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who though entitled to glory, emptied Himself as a servant, Philippians 2:6-7.

study.christ: The charge to open the mouth for the voiceless and defend the cause of the poor and needy, verses 8-9, is fulfilled perfectly in Jesus, who announced His own ministry in the words of Isaiah, anointed to preach good news to the poor and proclaim freedom for the oppressed, Luke 4:18, and who consistently spent His authority on behalf of those who had none of their own. And where this oracle warns a king against squandering his strength on self-indulgence, verses 3-5, Christ stands as the ultimate reversal, a true King who, though entitled to every glory, emptied Himself, took the form of a servant, and poured out His strength not on Himself but for others, Philippians 2:6-7.
study.god: God's own heart for the voiceless, the poor, and the needy stands behind this mother's charge to her royal son, since throughout Scripture God hears the cry of the oppressed and holds every ruler accountable for how the powerless are treated under their authority. He also cares that leaders maintain personal integrity, strength rightly used, sobriety, and self-control, since character and public justice can never be finally separated.

crossReferences.note: Christ's mission to preach good news to the poor, fulfilling Proverbs 31:8-9.
Christ emptied Himself rather than indulging Himself, the perfect answer to Proverbs 31:3-5.
A king's calling to defend the afflicted and needy, echoing Proverbs 31:8-9.
Defend the cause of the fatherless and plead the widow's case, echoing Proverbs 31:9.

study.oneStory: A mother's urgent charge to her royal son to guard his strength, remain sober, and above all use his voice for the voiceless sketches the pattern of righteous kingship the whole Bible longs for. That longing is answered in Jesus, the true King who did not squander His glory in self-indulgence but poured Himself out to bring justice and good news to the poor.

quiz.q: Who instructed King Lemuel according to verse 1?

options.text: His mother.
options.note: This oracle is explicitly credited to a queen mother's teaching.

options.text: His father, the previous king.
options.note: The text names his mother, not his father, as the source of this instruction.

options.text: A court prophet.
options.note: No prophet is named as the source; the instruction is maternal.

options.text: An unnamed group of royal advisors.
options.note: The text specifically credits one person, his mother, not a group of advisors.

quiz.q: What warning does verse 4-5 give a king about wine?

options.text: It is not for kings to drink wine, lest they forget what is decreed and deprive the oppressed of justice.
options.note: A king's clarity of mind is directly tied to protecting justice for the vulnerable.

options.text: Kings should drink freely to relax from the burdens of rule.
options.note: This is the opposite of the warning given.

options.text: Wine is forbidden for everyone, not just kings.
options.note: The warning here specifically addresses kings and rulers, not a universal prohibition.

options.text: Wine is acceptable for kings only during festivals.
options.note: The text gives no such festival exception; its concern is the king's ongoing responsibility for justice.

quiz.q: What does verse 8 charge the king to do?

options.text: Open his mouth for those with no voice, for the cause of all the dispossessed.
options.note: The king's authority is to be used specifically on behalf of the voiceless.

options.text: Remain silent on matters of justice to avoid controversy.
options.note: This is the opposite of the charge given.

options.text: Speak primarily in defense of his own royal interests.
options.note: The charge is explicitly to defend others, especially the dispossessed, not himself.

options.text: Delegate all matters of justice to lower officials.
options.note: The charge is a direct, personal responsibility given to the king himself, not a call to delegate it away.

quiz.q: What does verse 9 add to the charge of opening the mouth?

options.text: Judge righteously, and defend the cause of the poor and needy.
options.note: Righteous judgment and active defense of the vulnerable are paired together.

options.text: Judge according to the wealth of those involved.
options.note: This is the opposite of righteous judgment as described.

options.text: Avoid making any judgments at all.
options.note: The verse specifically calls for judging, not avoiding judgment.

options.text: Judge quickly without full consideration of the facts.
options.note: The verse calls for righteous judgment, which implies careful fairness, not mere speed.

quiz.q: What does verse 3 warn against regarding women?

options.text: Spending your strength on women who ruin kings.
options.note: The warning targets destructive relationships that corrupt a king's strength and rule, not women generally.

options.text: Ever marrying at all.
options.note: This is not the point of the warning; marriage itself is not condemned here.

options.text: Listening to any counsel from women.
options.note: This oracle is itself maternal counsel that the king is meant to heed, contradicting this option.

options.text: Having daughters as heirs.
options.note: This is not the subject of the verse; the warning concerns self-destructive indulgence, not succession.

questions: This oracle is a mother's urgent instruction to her royal son. What wisdom from a parent or mentor have you received that still shapes you today?
Verses 4-5 warn that a king's indulgence directly harms the oppressed who depend on his justice. Where might your own private habits be quietly affecting your ability to serve others well?
Verse 8 charges the king to open his mouth for those with no voice. Who around you currently has no voice, and what would it look like for you to speak up on their behalf?
Verse 9 calls for judging righteously and defending the poor and needy. Where in your own sphere of influence, family, work, community, are you being called to greater fairness?
This whole oracle points forward to Christ, the King who emptied Himself for others rather than indulging Himself. Where do you need to trade self-indulgence for self-giving this week?

translationNotes.note: 'The burden' translates massa, which can mean either a prophetic oracle or, following an alternate vocalization favored by some scholars, could relate to a place name, Massa, mentioned in Genesis 25:14; most translations, including the BSB, favor the sense of a solemn oracle or utterance here.

'Son of my vows' likely refers to a vow the mother made to God concerning this child, perhaps similar to Hannah's vow concerning Samuel, 1 Samuel 1:11, underscoring the weight and seriousness of her instruction.

This exception for giving strong drink to the perishing stands in deliberate contrast to the king's own required sobriety in verses 4-5, sharpening rather than softening the warning against royal self-indulgence.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit for Proverbs 31:1-9. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
